title: Poker’s biggest tournament is headed back to ESPN.
body: The Main Event begins July 2. The final table returns August 3–5 for a three-night live finale.

title: Texas Hold’em works on TV because the audience knows the secret.
body: The player has two cards. The viewer has the story. The suspense lives in the gap between them.

title: 100 bracelets. One world championship.
body: The 2026 WSOP runs May 26–July 15 at Horseshoe and Paris Las Vegas, with the $10K No-Limit Hold’em Main Event as the centerpiece.

title: The WSOP brand sold for $500 million.
body: Caesars closed the WSOP brand sale to NSUS Group in 2024 while retaining the right to host the summer series in Las Vegas for 20 years.

title: Main Event calendar
body: July 2: cards in the air. July 13: final table set. August 3–5: ESPN finale. Poker gets a cliffhanger again.

title: The Main Event is still enormous.
body: 2023: 10,043 entries. 2024: 10,112 entries. 2025: 9,735 entries. The fields are no longer a nostalgia act.

title: The dream still has a name: Moneymaker.
body: In 2003, Chris Moneymaker turned an online satellite seat into a Main Event title and became poker’s cleanest mass-market myth.

title: The clean viewing path
body: Follow Day 1A on July 2, learn the survivors by Day 6, meet the final nine after July 13, then watch the August 3–5 finish.

title: The player in the chair has to make the decision.
body: No charts, no apps, no AI help, no rail-coaching loopholes. The television product needs trust as much as drama.

title: A tank is not dead air if the stakes are clear.
body: A tank is a long decision. Good poker TV makes it suspense. Bad poker TV makes it feel like someone forgot to edit.

title: Poker is not really about cards.
body: It is about what people do when they do not know enough — but still have to choose.

title: Question for the rail
body: Does ESPN’s return make poker feel mainstream again, or is the game too fragmented now for one final table to pull everyone back?
